title: Today's Tech Brief: Lego’s Smart Play Pokémon can train and battle, but don’t do the one thing I wish t...
summary: A look at Lego's new Smart Play Pokémon sets and Google's Gemini Spark AI experience.
imageAlt: Image from theverge.com for Lego’s Smart Play Pokémon can train and battle, but don’t do the one thing I wish they could
imageCaption: Lego’s Smart Play Pokémon can train and battle, but don’t do the one thing I wish they could (theverge.com)

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's Tech Brief. I've got two interesting tech stories for you today. First up, Lego has launched its new Smart Play Pokémon sets, which let kids train and battle their favorite Pokémon characters. But, there's a catch - or rather, a missing feature that has me scratching my head.

According to The Verge, these new Lego sets use a smart technology that allows kids to train and battle their Pokémon. Sounds like a blast, right? However, the one thing that's noticeably missing is the ability for the Pokémon to utter their iconic catchphrases. I mean, who doesn't want to hear Pikachu say 'pika pika'? It's a small thing, but it feels like a missed opportunity to really bring these characters to life.

I've been thinking about these sets, and I wonder if it's a case of trying to keep things simple or if it's just a oversight. Either way, it's an interesting example of how tech can enhance playtime, but also how some things are still best left to our imaginations.

Gemini Spark is the most impressive and terrifying AI experience I’ve had yet, from theverge.com, is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: AI Tech Google Gemini Spark is the most impressive and terrifying AI experience I’ve had yet It’s a remarkable piece of technology. But the future sure is creepy. by David Pierce Jun 2, 2026, 12:38 PM UTC Spark is Google’s new agentic answer for everything. | Image: Google AI Tech Google Gemini Spark is the most impressive and terrifying AI experience I’ve ... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.
